eli3: The chi-square test asks: are categorical variables independent? If you survey people about their political affiliation and their preferred news source, is there a relationship — or are they independent? Chi-square compares the observed cell counts in a contingency table to the counts you would expect if the variables were independent.

Expected count for a cell = (row total × column total) / grand total. The chi-square statistic sums up (observed − expected)²/expected across all cells. If the variables are truly independent, this should be small. If it is large, we reject independence.

The chi-square distribution is the distribution of the sum of squared standard normals: if Z₁,…,Zₖ ~ N(0,1) independently, then Z₁²+…+Zₖ² ~ χ²(k). For a contingency table with r rows and c columns, the test statistic follows χ²((r−1)(c−1)) under the null of independence.

whenWhy.title: Testing independence between categorical variables
whenWhy.body: Chi-square test of independence: are two categorical variables related? Examples: is treatment assignment independent of patient outcome? Is product category independent of region? This is one of the most commonly used tests in survey analysis, clinical trials, and A/B tests with categorical outcomes.

whenWhy.title: Goodness-of-fit: does data fit a hypothesised distribution?
whenWhy.body: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test: H₀: data follows a specified distribution. Compare observed frequencies to expected frequencies under H₀. Used for: testing if dice are fair, testing if word counts follow Zipf's law, or testing if a feature follows a normal distribution (after binning).

whenWhy.title: Feature selection for categorical predictors
whenWhy.body: In ML, chi-square test is used for feature selection with categorical features and categorical targets. Features with low χ² (p-value > threshold) are approximately independent of the target and may be dropped. Scikit-learn's chi2 selector implements this for text classification (word frequency vs class label).

whenWhy.title: Model calibration and distribution testing
whenWhy.body: After fitting a model, chi-square tests compare observed vs model-predicted frequencies across bins (Hosmer-Lemeshow test for logistic regression calibration). High χ² indicates poor model fit. Also used to test if residuals follow the hypothesised distribution.

deepDiveIntro: For a contingency table with r rows and c columns, the expected count in each cell is the product of that cell's row total and column total divided by the grand total. The chi-square statistic sums the squared difference between observed and expected over expected, across all cells. Under the null hypothesis of independence, this statistic follows a chi-square distribution with (r−1)(c−1) degrees of freedom as the sample size grows (Pearson, 1900).

The chi-square test is a large-sample approximation. It is reliable when all expected cell counts are at least 5 (a common rule of thumb). For small expected counts — especially in 2×2 tables — use Fisher's exact test instead.

deepDive.heading: Pearson Chi-Square Statistic
deepDive.body: The expected count for each cell is the row total times the column total divided by the grand total — this is what you would expect if the row and column categories were completely unrelated. The chi-square statistic accumulates the standardized squared deviation between what was observed and what was expected across every cell. The degrees of freedom are (r−1)(c−1): one constraint per row marginal, one per column marginal, and one for the fixed grand total. Reject independence if the statistic exceeds the critical value for your chosen significance level. [1]

deepDive.heading: Chi-Square Distribution
deepDive.body: The chi-square distribution with k degrees of freedom is the sum of k squared independent standard normal variables. Its mean is k and variance is 2k. It is right-skewed for small k and becomes approximately normal for large k. It is always non-negative because it is a sum of squares. Common critical values: for 1 degree of freedom at the 5% level the threshold is 3.84; for 2 it is 5.99; for 3 it is 7.81. [1]

deepDive.heading: Goodness-of-Fit Test
deepDive.body: For a goodness-of-fit test, you compare observed counts in k bins to the counts you would expect under a hypothesized distribution. The degrees of freedom are k minus 1 minus the number of parameters estimated from the data. Estimating parameters from the data uses up degrees of freedom because the expected counts become less independent of the observed data. For example, testing normality after estimating both the mean and standard deviation from the data costs 2 degrees of freedom. [1]
deepDive.formula: χ² = Σᵢ (Oᵢ − Eᵢ)² / Eᵢ  ~  χ²(k − 1 − m)

deepDive.heading: Effect Size: Cramér's V
deepDive.body: The chi-square statistic is not a measure of effect size — it grows with sample size even for a fixed association. Cramér's V divides by the sample size and the smaller of (rows−1) and (columns−1) to produce a number between 0 (complete independence) and 1 (perfect association). For a 2×2 table, Cramér's V equals the phi coefficient. Cohen's rough benchmarks: V near 0.1 is small, 0.3 is medium, 0.5 is large. [1][2]

takeaways: χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E with E = row_total × col_total / n; ~ χ²((r−1)(c−1)) under independence. [1]
Valid when all expected counts Eᵢⱼ ≥ 5; use Fisher's exact test for small cells (2×2 tables). [1]
Goodness-of-fit: χ² ~ χ²(k−1−m) where m = parameters estimated; accounts for df used fitting. [1]
χ² increases with n for fixed association — use Cramér's V for effect size, not χ² alone. [1][2]
Chi-square test is asymptotic (large-sample); for small expected counts prefer exact tests. [1]
Used in ML feature selection: chi2 test selects categorical features most associated with target. [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you use Fisher's exact test instead of chi-square?
edgeCases.a: Use Fisher's exact test for 2×2 tables when any expected count Eᵢⱼ < 5 (or sample size n < 20). Fisher's test conditions on the marginal totals being fixed and computes the exact hypergeometric probability of the observed or more extreme tables. It is computationally feasible for 2×2 tables; for larger tables, exact tests become expensive and approximate chi-square is usually sufficient if n is large. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why do we subtract the number of estimated parameters from the degrees of freedom in the goodness-of-fit test?
edgeCases.a: Each parameter estimated from the data 'uses up' one degree of freedom — the expected counts Eᵢ become dependent on the data, reducing the effective number of free comparisons. Failing to subtract m makes the test too liberal (rejects too often). For example, testing normality after estimating μ̂ and σ̂ from the same data: df = k − 1 − 2 = k − 3, not k − 1. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 10
Agresti — An Introduction to Categorical Data Analysis (3rd ed.), Ch. 2
